content.text: A change champion is a peer-nominated or self-selected employee who serves as the programme's internal advocate within their team. They are distinct from executive sponsors (who hold formal power) and from the AI delivery team (who build the product) — their value comes from credibility with the colleagues who will actually use the AI. In large deployments covering hundreds of users, the delivery team cannot be the primary adoption driver for every team; champions extend that capacity. In smaller deployments, a single well-placed champion can shift an entire team from neutral to engaged. Change champion identification and management is a structured discipline, not a passive call for volunteers.

attrs.alt: Change champion lifecycle: Identify, Enable, Activate, Sustain

content.text: What Makes an Effective Change Champion

Not every engaged employee makes an effective change champion. The role requires a specific combination of attributes that are distinct from technical aptitude or seniority. Four dimensions predict champion effectiveness:

Peer credibility is the most important dimension — it is the reason colleagues listen to the champion rather than dismissing their advocacy as top-down messaging. Credibility comes from track record: colleagues trust the champion's judgement because they have seen it validated in the past. Credibility is role- and context-specific: the most credible senior adjuster in the motor claims team may have zero credibility with the property claims team.

— Colleagues seek this person out when they have a question or a problem, even when they are not their manager; they are the informal 'go-to' person for work queries in their team; they are trusted to give honest opinions, not politically safe answers.
— Recently joined the team (< 6 months); known as a management ally rather than a peer voice; previously advocated for a change that the team experienced negatively.

— Line manager nominations weighted by specificity ('she is always the first person the team asks when they're stuck on a complex claim') are more reliable than generic positive descriptions ('he is a great team player').

A champion who cannot accurately describe how the AI changes their own workflow cannot convincingly field questions from sceptical peers. Domain expertise means the champion understands the work being changed deeply enough to explain the impact at a task level: 'the AI handles the initial categorisation; I now spend that time on coverage questions where adjuster judgement matters'.
— The champion should have been doing the impacted work for at least 12 months and have a clear mental model of which tasks are changing and which are staying the same.

— A team lead who has moved away from case-level work may have high seniority but low task-level expertise. A senior adjuster who still handles complex cases daily may be a better domain expert for the AI's specific impact.

Change openness is the champion's personal comfort with new tools, processes, and ways of working. A champion who privately shares the team's scepticism will not sustain their advocacy under social pressure. This is not the same as naïve enthusiasm — a champion who asks hard questions of the delivery team in private but advocates confidently in public once those questions are answered is more effective than one who has never examined their own assumptions.
— Has adopted new tools or processes before peers in previous initiatives; comfortable with uncertainty and iterative change; willing to be seen failing publicly while learning new technology.
— 'I support AI in principle but our situation is different' — this framing usually predicts low change openness under pressure from sceptical peers.

The champion must be able to explain the AI's capability and limitations clearly to non-technical colleagues, run an informal demo, and handle objections without dismissing them. Communication skill is weighted lowest because it is the most trainable dimension — a credible domain expert with moderate communication skills can be prepared with structured talking points and demo scripts. Peer credibility and domain expertise are much harder to develop on programme timescales.
Three methods are used in combination to build the champion candidate list:
Managers in impacted teams nominate 1–2 candidates from their team based on the four criteria
Managers have direct visibility of peer credibility and domain expertise; nomination creates early manager buy-in
Managers may nominate compliant employees rather than genuinely credible ones; avoid telling managers that 'champions support the programme' as a selection criterion
Programme communicates the champion role to all impacted employees with a short application form covering the four criteria
Captures motivated candidates managers might overlook; signals that the programme values employee participation
Self-selected candidates may have high change openness but low peer credibility; requires screening
AI team identifies informal leaders through stakeholder interviews, discovery workshops, and pilot observation
Captures employees who would be highly effective but would not self-nominate; often the most credible candidates
Time-intensive; requires the AI team to spend time in the operation before they know the candidates

The most common identification mistake is selecting champions from existing change management volunteers or 'innovation club' members. These self-selected groups often have high change openness but moderate peer credibility — they are seen by sceptical colleagues as 'management-aligned' even when they are not. Prioritise credibility over enthusiasm.

Champions cannot advocate for what they do not understand. The enablement programme gives champions access to the AI product, the evidence base, and the key messages before the pilot launch — typically 2–4 weeks ahead of the broader rollout:

— Champions get access to the AI tool before other team members. They use it on real work (not synthetic examples) to build genuine familiarity and identify limitations. The delivery team should monitor early champion usage and proactively answer questions that arise.

— For use cases where the quality of the AI's output depends on how the user frames their input, champions need working-level prompt skills. This is not a general AI literacy session — it is targeted on the specific tool and the tasks the champion will use it for. Run by the AI team with the actual product interface.

— Share the evaluation results: accuracy rates, error patterns, tasks where the AI performs well and tasks where it does not. Champions who understand the evidence can give honest, credible answers to 'but is it actually accurate?' rather than defaulting to programme messaging. Sharing limitations builds more credibility than overselling accuracy.

— Structured talking points for the most common objections the champion will face in their team. Tailored by team and role — what the motor claims champion needs to say is different from what the liability team champion needs to say. Include: what changes, what stays the same, what happens if the AI is wrong, how to give feedback.

— Champions need a fast-response channel to the AI team for questions they cannot answer. A champion who says 'I don't know, but I'll find out and get back to you tomorrow' and delivers on that promise builds more credibility than one who deflects. A dedicated Slack channel or email alias with a 4-hour response SLA is sufficient.

The activation phase is when champions move from prepared advocates to active participants in the deployment. Effective activation gives champions specific roles rather than a general mandate to 'support the rollout':
Champion is in the first cohort of users; provides structured feedback via champion log
Champion runs a 30-minute demo for their team using real examples from their own work
All enablement sessions + key message prep
Champion is the named first point of contact for team members' questions about the AI
Champion conducts informal pulse checks with team members weekly and escalates emerging concerns to the delivery team
Structured feedback template provided by delivery team
Champion connects their team's experiences with champions from other teams; attends champion community of practice
Community of practice format established by Change Lead

Not every champion needs to fulfil all roles. In a small team (8 people), one champion handling all five roles is manageable. In a large deployment (200 users), a network of 10 champions with divided responsibilities is more sustainable. Assign roles explicitly — a champion without a specific role becomes a passive endorser, not an active advocate.
Champion Sizing: How Many Champions Do You Need?

Champion network sizing determines how much peer influence capacity the programme has relative to the size of the impacted population. Too few champions means coverage gaps where teams have no peer advocate; too many creates coordination overhead and dilutes the identity and prestige of the role.
Single champion supported directly by AI lead and Change Lead
Small cohort with monthly community of practice; each champion manages 1–2 roles
Structured network with team champions, senior champions (1 per cluster of teams), and a champion lead role (0.5 FTE)
1 per 20 users minimum; add dedicated Champion Lead role
Hierarchical: champion lead → cluster champions → team champions; programme office provides coordination support

These ratios assume champions are handling 2–4 hours per week of champion activities. For complex deployments with high scepticism, high change impact, or regulated environments requiring individual guidance (rather than group communication), the ratio should be 1:10–1:15 rather than 1:20–1:25.
Coverage Prioritisation When Budget Is Constrained
When the number of available champions is limited, prioritise coverage by combining impact and resistance:
— Teams where the AI change is large (significant task change) and the team is already sceptical. Assign the highest-scoring champions here first.
— Teams where the change is large but the team is receptive. These teams benefit from champions but will likely adopt without one; assign second priority.
— Teams where the AI touches only a small part of the work but where one sceptical team lead could generate disproportionate negative narrative. Assign a credible champion even with limited capacity.
— These teams can be covered by programme-wide communication (newsletter, town hall) without dedicated champions.

A champion in the wrong team — one with low impact and low resistance — provides less value than a champion in a sceptical, high-impact team. Map champion placement to where peer influence will have the greatest effect on adoption outcomes, not to where it is easiest to place.

Champion networks are frequently created for the pilot and then abandoned at production deployment — the moment when peer influence matters most. Sustaining the network through the first 90 days of production operation is critical because this is when adoption either consolidates or degrades:

— The most common reason champions disengage post-deployment is that their champion activities were treated as additional to their full job load. By deployment, champion activities should be reflected in workload planning: 2–4 hours per week as a named commitment rather than informal extra effort. This requires line manager buy-in — the line manager must understand and protect the capacity.

— A monthly forum where champions across all impacted teams share what is working, what is not, and what emerging questions they are hearing from colleagues. This community provides peer support for champions (who are also navigating change in their own work) and surfaces adoption signals that would otherwise take longer to reach the delivery team.

— Champion contributions should be recognised in the ways that matter in the organisation's culture: visible acknowledgement by the executive sponsor at a steering committee; inclusion in the programme's success story; career development conversations that reference the change leadership role. Recognition that feels performative to the champion will not sustain engagement.

— Champions who become deeply embedded in the AI tool's operation sometimes move into formal AI roles (prompt engineering, evaluation, CoE liaison). When this happens, their champion role in the original team becomes vacant. Plan for champion turnover and identify successors before the role becomes empty.
Worked Example: FNOL Tool Champion Network Design
A general insurer deploying an AI-assisted FNOL classification tool across 180 adjusters in four claims teams (Motor, Property, Liability, Travel) designs its champion network as follows:
Priya Sharma (Senior Adj.) + Kwame Asante (Team Lead)
Priya: peer credibility; Kwame: formal authority
Kwame may be seen as management proxy — monitor peer response
Yuki: highest domain expertise in CAT claims edge cases
Highest peer credibility score in candidate pool
Team is most sceptical of AI classification accuracy
Self-nominated with strong evidence of change openness
Recently joined team (8 months) — credibility still building
Champions identified; line managers briefed on capacity protection (2h/week minimum)
Product access granted; champions use tool on 10–15 real cases from their own queue
Prompt engineering workshop (90min): effective framing for FNOL tool inputs
Evidence briefing: accuracy results by claim type (including where tool performs poorly)
Key message preparation: objection handling by team, tailored to each team's known concerns
Champion demo rehearsals: each champion runs a 15-minute practice demo with AI team feedback
Champions run team demos on launch day; peer Q&A role activated
First champion community of practice: share early signals, agree talking points for emerging questions

The liability claims team (most sceptical) is assigned the highest-scoring champion (Fatima, 4.4). The travel claims team is flagged as moderate risk because Tom's credibility is still establishing — the Change Lead schedules additional support for this team in weeks 2–4 of the pilot.
Common Failure Modes in Champion Networks

— The champion who is most excited about AI in discovery workshops is often the team member with the lowest peer credibility. Sceptical colleagues distrust enthusiasm. The most effective champions are often those who asked the hard questions in the discovery workshop and had those questions answered satisfactorily — their advocacy is more credible because their scepticism is known.
No capacity protection from line managers

— Champions who must perform their full job plus champion activities will reduce champion activities under workload pressure. This happens predictably at two points: 3–4 weeks into the pilot (novelty wears off, workload reasserts) and at production go-live (everyone's attention is on the launch). Capacity protection must be agreed with line managers in writing before the programme begins, not requested informally.

— Adoption consolidates or degrades in the 30–90 days after go-live. The programme team frequently disbands the champion structure at go-live because 'delivery is complete'. This is the highest-risk period for adoption dropout. Sustain the network for a minimum of 90 days post-deployment with monthly community of practice and quarterly check-ins thereafter.
Champions become the product support team

— When the delivery team does not provide adequate user support, colleagues route all product questions through the champion. Champions who spend 6+ hours per week answering product questions burn out quickly and typically disengage within 6–8 weeks. The champion role is advocacy and peer connection, not first-line support. Ensure the product has a proper support channel (helpdesk, FAQ, direct AI team contact) so champions are not the last resort.
No champion for a sceptical sub-group

— If the most vocal sceptics in the team are a specific sub-group (e.g. senior adjusters handling complex cases, who fear that AI accuracy on edge cases does not meet their professional standards), the champion programme must include a credible member of that sub-group. A champion who is only credible with junior adjusters will not move the sceptical senior cohort.

Effective change champions combine peer credibility (35% weight), domain expertise (25%), change openness (25%), and communication skill (15%). Credibility is the most important and least trainable dimension — prioritise it over enthusiasm or availability.

Use three identification methods in combination: line manager nominations (with structured criteria), self-nomination (with screening), and observational identification during discovery and workshops.

The enablement programme gives champions product access, evidence briefing, prompt engineering skills, key message preparation, and a direct CoE line — typically 2–4 weeks before the wider pilot launch.

Activate champions in specific, named roles during the pilot: first-wave user, team demo facilitator, peer Q&A responder, feedback collector, cross-team connector. A general mandate to 'support the rollout' does not produce consistent champion behaviour.

Sustain the network post-deployment with capacity protection (2–4h/week in workload planning), a monthly community of practice, recognition aligned to organisational culture, and succession planning for champion turnover.
